title: A broke clerk earned a fortune on every copy ever made #Shorts
desc: Chester Carlson worked from the age of eight and left college fifteen hundred dollars in debt, then wrote to 82 companies for a job and got zero offers. Stuck copying patents by hand, he built the first dry photocopy in a rented room in Queens on October 22, 1938 — and was turned down by more than 20 companies, including IBM and the U.S. Navy. When his idea finally became the Xerox 914 office copier, he earned a fraction of a cent on every copy made worldwide, became one of the richest men in America, and quietly gave away over 150 million dollars.
